title: From $500K HDB to $1.8M condo in 5 years
subtitle: A staircase-wealth playbook
standfirst: Idris and Mira moved from a Punggol HDB to a $1.8M condo in two deliberate moves. Their income barely grew. The asset class did all the heavy lifting.
description: How Idris and Mira used equity recycling to move from a Punggol HDB to a $1.8M condo in two moves — with minimal income growth.

clients.incomeNote: Minimal income growth over the period — wealth built through asset moves, not salary

moves.title: HDB → First Condo

rows.label: Bought
rows.value: 3-bedder condo (ground floor — only unit within budget)

rows.label: Cash deployment
rows.value: ~50% of cash profits ($65K) for cash portion + stamp duty. Remainder to renovation.

moves.keyDecision: Didn't wait for the 'perfect' unit. Got into the condo market with what they could afford.

moves.title: First Condo → Second Condo

rows.label: Cash deployment
rows.value: Portion of $350K profit funded the cash portion of the next property

moves.keyDecision: Exited precisely at SSD expiry. Recycled the profit into a stronger asset class.

rows.label: Key insight
rows.value: Income didn't grow dramatically. The asset class did all the heavy lifting.

problem: HDB owners who have completed MOP and want to upgrade but feel stuck — they think they need higher income or more savings to make a meaningful move into private property.
mistake: Sit in the HDB, pay it off, feel "safe" — and miss the equity-building window entirely. Or wait for the "perfect" condo (high floor, good facing, ideal location) and never make the first move.
whatElfiDid: Identified that the first move didn't need to be perfect — it needed to be strategic. A ground-floor 3-bedder was enough to get into the condo market and start building equity.
Structured the cash deployment: 50% of HDB profits into the condo purchase, the rest into renovation. No overextension.
Timed the exit from the first condo precisely at SSD expiry (4 years).
Recycled the $350K profit into the next, stronger asset ($1.6M condo).
Each move was a burst of wealth — not gradual appreciation, but a concentrated jump in asset class.
result: Two bursts of profit: $130K (HDB sale) → $350K (first condo sale)
Each burst funded the next move — no external cash injection needed beyond the original HDB equity
Income stayed relatively flat — this was purely an asset-class play

frameworks.name: Staircase Wealth / Equity Recycling
frameworks.description: Profits from each sale become the fuel for the next, stronger property. HDB profit → first condo → first condo profit → second condo.

frameworks.name: Quantum Positioning
frameworks.description: Entry beats perfection. The first condo didn't need to be the dream home — the point was entering the condo market and starting the equity clock.

frameworks.name: SSD Timing
frameworks.description: Planned the exit around the 4-year SSD expiry. Selling even one month early would have cost thousands in stamp duty.

frameworks.name: Leverage Amplification
frameworks.description: Used mortgage leverage to control assets worth far more than their cash position. $65K cash → controlled a condo that appreciated $350K.

frameworks.name: Next Better Property
frameworks.description: Each move was deliberately into a stronger asset class, not a lateral move. The $1.6M condo is now worth $1.8M+ — the third burst is already building.

frameworks.name: Burst Framework
frameworks.description: Wealth didn't grow linearly at 3% per year. It grew in two sharp bursts — $130K and $350K — each triggered by a deliberate move, not passive appreciation.

takeaway: If your HDB equity is sitting idle, you're not being safe — you're renting inflation on your own capital. One well-timed move can reset your trajectory for a decade.
